fields.label: Risk-Free Rate %
fields.help: Annual interest rate expressed as a percentage.

explanation:
**Personal finance and investing** use universal mathematical principles (compound interest, time value of money, risk-return optimization). This calculator applies standard financial industry formulas.

## How It's Calculated

The Sharpe ratio of a trading strategy measures risk-adjusted returns.

This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting to unreasonable values.

## Typical Benchmarks by Timeframe

| Timeframe | S&P 500 | 10-Year Bonds | Cash |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 year | Highly variable | 4–5% | 3–5% |
| 5 years | ~8% average | ~4% | Inflation |
| 10 years | ~9–10% | ~4% | <inflation |
| 20+ years | ~10% nominal | ~5% | N/A |

## Investment Principles

- **Don't abandon the plan** during market crises. Emotion is your worst advisor.
- **Diversify**: don't concentrate in a single asset, sector, or country.
- **Keep costs low**: expense ratios >1% annually eat 30% of your returns over 30 years.
- **Long time horizon**: stocks work over 10+ years, not 1 year.
- **Annual rebalancing**: once per year, nothing more.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are updated to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: It measures risk-adjusted returns: how much profit you generate per unit of volatility. Higher is better.

faq.q: How do I interpret the Sharpe ratio?
faq.a: >1: decent. >2: very good. >3: exceptional. Most real-world strategies fall below 1.

faq.q: Is a low Sharpe ratio bad?
faq.a: <1: returns don't compensate for the risk. Your strategy may need improvement.

faq.q: What are the limitations of Sharpe ratio?
faq.a: Assumes normal distribution of returns. Outliers and fat tails break the model. Use with other metrics.
A variant that measures only downside volatility—often more useful than Sharpe for real trading.

faq.q: How can I improve my Sharpe ratio?
faq.a: Lower volatility (stop losses, diversification) or increase returns (better alpha or edge).

faq.q: What was the S&P 500's historical Sharpe ratio?
faq.a: Approximately 0.5–0.7 Sharpe over the last 50 years, depending on the period.

faq.q: What stock market returns should I expect?
faq.a: S&P 500 historical average: ~10% nominal annually, ~7% real (inflation-adjusted). Past performance does not guarantee future results.

faq.q: Can I use Sharpe ratio for crypto trading?
faq.a: Yes, but cryptocurrency has extreme volatility and fat tails. Consider Sortino ratio or other risk metrics as well.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
